analogy: Think of "Money and Credit" through the chapter's own opening images: NOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER 3 : MONEY AND CREDIT Money is a fascinating subject and full of curiosities. It is important to capture this element for the students. The history of money and how various forms were used at different times is an interesting... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Money and Credit is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that nOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER 3 : MONEY AND CREDIT Money is a fascinating subject and full of curiosities.

The text explains that it is important to capture this element for the students.

The text explains that the history of money and how various forms were used at different times is an interesting story.

The text explains that at this stage the purpose is to allow students to realise the social situation in which these forms were used.

The text explains that modern forms of money are linked to the banking system.

The text explains that this is the central idea of the first part of the chapter.

The text explains that the present situation in India, where newer forms of money are slowly spreading with computerisation of the banking system, offers many opportunities to students to explore on their own.

The text explains that we need not get into a formal discussion of the ‘functions of money’ but let it come up as questions.

The text explains that as you would see in the chapter, the stock of money consists of currency held by the public and the demand deposits that they hold with the banks.

The text explains that this is the money that people can use as they wish and the government has to ensure that the system works smoothy.

The text explains that what would happen when the government declares that some of the currency notes used by people would be made invalid and would be replaced by new currency?

The text explains that in India, during November 2016, currency notes in the denomination of Rs.

The text explains that people were asked to surrender these notes to the bank by a specific period and receive new Rs.

The text explains that since then, people were also encouraged to use their bank deposits rather than cash for transactions.

The text explains that hence, digital transactions started by using bank-to-bank transfer through the internet or mobile phones, cheques, ATM cards, credit cards, Point of Sale (POS) swipe machines, and QR codes through UPI system at shops.

The text explains that this is promoted to reduce the requirement of cash for transactions and also control corruption.

The text explains that sources for Information The data on formal and informal sector credit used in this chapter is drawn from the survey on rural debt by the National Sample Survey Organisation (All India Debt and Investment Survey, 77th Round 2019, conducted by NSSO) now known as National Statistical Office (NSO).

The text explains that imagine how much more difficult it would be if the shoe manufacturer had to directly exchange shoes for wheat without the use of money.

The text explains that in a barter system where goods are directly exchanged without the use of money, double coincidence of wants is an essential feature.

The text explains that the facility of cheques against demand deposits makes it possible to directly settle payments without the use of cash.

The text explains that the difference between what is charged from borrowers and what is paid to depositors is their main source of income.

The text explains that to meet these expenses, Salim obtains loans from two sources.

The text explains that we head directly for the fields where most farmers and labourers would be working at this time of the day.

The text explains that rama tells us that the only source of credit for the landless people in Sonpur are the landowner-employers.

The text explains that loans from Cooperatives Loans from Cooperatives Loans from Cooperatives Loans from Cooperatives Loans from Cooperatives Besides banks, the other major source of cheap credit in rural areas are the cooperative societies (or cooperatives).

The text explains that members of a cooperative pool their resources for cooperation in certain areas.

The text explains that list the various sources of credit in Sonpur.

The text explains that they can lend at whatever interest rate they We have seen in the above examples that people obtain loans from various sources.

The text explains that in Graph 1 you can see the various sources of credit to rural households in India.

The text explains that the Reserve Bank of India supervises the functioning of formal sources of loans.

The text explains that graph 1 : Sources of Credit in Rural India, 2019 Other Informal Agencies, 3% Cooperative Banks and Society, 10% Relatives and Friends, 7% Landlords, 1% Commercial Banks, 51% Moneylenders, 23% Other Formal Agencies, 5%

The text explains that graph 2 shows the importance of formal and informal sources of credit for people in urban areas.

The text explains that you can see that 54 per cent of the loans taken by poor households in the urban areas are from informal sources.

The text explains that only 17 per cent of their loans are from informal sources, while 83 per cent are from formal sources.

The text explains that the remaining credit needs are met from informal sources.

The text explains that thus, it is necessary that banks and cooperatives increase their lending particularly in the rural areas, so that the dependence on informal sources of credit reduces.

The text explains that what are the differences between formal and informal sources of credit?

The text explains that at present, it is the richer households who receive formal credit whereas the poor have to depend on the informal sources.

The text explains that in the previous section we have seen that poor households are still dependent on informal sources of credit.

The text explains that even when they are present, getting a loan from a bank is much more difficult than taking a loan from informal sources.

The text explains that credit is available from a variety of sources.

The text explains that these can be either formal sources or informal sources.

The text explains that at present, it is the richer households who receive credit from formal sources whereas the poor have to depend on the informal sources.

The text explains that why do we need to expand formal sources of credit in India?

The text explains that (b) What are the other sources from which the small farmers can borrow?

The text explains that fill in the blanks: (i) Majority of the credit needs of the _________________households are met from informal sources.

The text explains that (ii) Formal sources of credit does not include (a) Banks.

The text explains that (iii) __________________ issues currency notes on behalf of the Central Government.

The text explains that (iv) Banks charge a higher interest rate on loans than what they offer on __________.

The text explains that (v) _______________ is an asset that the borrower owns and uses as a guarantee until the loan is repaid to the lender.

The text explains that (i) In a SHG most of the decisions regarding savings and loan activities are taken by (a) Bank.

The text explains that the following table shows people in a variety of occupations in urban areas.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Understanding Economic Development, Chapter 3: Money and Credit

prelims_traps: Do not treat NOTES FOR THE TEACHER and CHAPTER 3 : MONEY AND CREDIT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Money and Credit; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Money and Credit through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use NOTES FOR THE TEACHER and CHAPTER 3 : MONEY AND CREDIT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
